===============================================

Today is February 21, 2026. US President Donald Trump recently announced a new global tariff of 15% following a Supreme Court ruling. On what specific date are these new tariffs scheduled to come into force?
(A) Tuesday 24 February
(B) Friday 20 February
(C) Saturday 21 February
(D) Monday 23 February
(E) Wednesday 25 February
explanation: According to the article, the new 15% tariffs "will come into force on Tuesday 24 February."
model_output:
The Supreme Court issued a ruling on February 20, 2026, limiting presidential authority to impose tariffs under IEEPA. In response, President Trump announced a new global tariff under Section 122 of the 1974 Trade Act, initially at 10% but quickly increased to 15%. While announcements and adjustments occurred between February 20 and 22, the effective date for the 15% tariff is February 24, 2026, which is a Tuesday.

<final_answer>
(A)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- Trade Compliance Resource Hub (https://www.tradecomplianceresourcehub.com/2026/02/22/trump-2-0-tariff-tracker)[[1]](https://www.tradecomplianceresourcehub.com/2026/02/22/trump-2-0-tariff-tracker)
- CNN (https://www.cnn.com/us/live-news/trump-administration-tariffs-iran-news-02-21-26)[[2]](https://www.cnn.com/us/live-news/trump-administration-tariffs-iran-news-02-21-26)
- Supreme Court (https://www.supremecourt.gov/opinions/25pdf/24-1287_4gcj.pdf)[[3]](https://www.supremecourt.gov/opinions/25pdf/24-1287_4gcj.pdf)
- The Guardian (https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/feb/22/trump-tariff-policy-ruling)[[4]](https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/feb/22/trump-tariff-policy-ruling)
- Wincalendar (https://www.wincalendar.com/Calendar/Date/February-21-2026)[[5]](https://www.wincalendar.com/Calendar/Date/February-21-2026)
- White House (https://www.whitehouse.gov/fact-sheets/2026/02/fact-sheet-president-donald-j-trump-imposes-a-temporary-import-duty-to-address-fundamental-international-payment-problems)[[6]](https://www.whitehouse.gov/fact-sheets/2026/02/fact-sheet-president-donald-j-trump-imposes-a-temporary-import-duty-to-address-fundamental-international-payment-problems)

Today is February 21, 2026. President Trump has criticized the Supreme Court justices who voted to strike down his global tariffs. Which justice wrote the majority opinion for the 6-3 decision?
(A) Neil Gorsuch
(B) John Roberts
(C) Amy Coney Barrett
(D) Clarence Thomas
(E) Brett Kavanaugh
explanation: The article states that "Chief Justice John Roberts is a George W Bush pick who wrote the majority opinion."
model_output:
Based on recent news from February 20, 2026, the U.S. Supreme Court issued a 6-3 decision in the case of *Learning Resources, Inc. v. Trump* striking down President Trump's global tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA).[[1]](https://www.supremecourt.gov/opinions/25pdf/24-1287_4gcj.pdf) The majority opinion was authored by Chief Justice John Roberts, who was joined by the three liberal justices and two other conservatives in ruling that the tariffs exceeded presidential authority.[[2]](https://www.nytimes.com/live/2026/02/20/us/trump-tariffs-supreme-court)[[3]](https://www.wsj.com/us-news/law/trump-tariffs-supreme-court-decision-29c26fa2?gaa_at=eafs&gaa_n=AWEtsqc9pBiGLK5ZTro8_wrOnZNvhtRVmTc4lh8ysM4iR9gyfu0d-jjq-w5O&gaa_sig=dxyL6M8e7aEGm1fMSkWDfNUp4VwJ_K2PE6vFSlDLmyBpEWWmRDwlfZjo9bVoHLenMDqjmnFE-BZpGC-iXMpkZg%3D%3D&gaa_ts=699bd3a7) This aligns with Roberts' history of occasionally moderating conservative positions in high-profile cases involving executive power. President Trump has since publicly criticized the decision and the justices in the majority.

<final_answer>
(B)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- Supreme Court (https://www.supremecourt.gov/opinions/25pdf/24-1287_4gcj.pdf)
- The New York Times (https://www.nytimes.com/live/2026/02/20/us/trump-tariffs-supreme-court)
- The Wall Street Journal (https://www.wsj.com/us-news/law/trump-tariffs-supreme-court-decision-29c26fa2?gaa_at=eafs&gaa_n=AWEtsqc9pBiGLK5ZTro8_wrOnZNvhtRVmTc4lh8ysM4iR9gyfu0d-jjq-w5O&gaa_sig=dxyL6M8e7aEGm1fMSkWDfNUp4VwJ_K2PE6vFSlDLmyBpEWWmRDwlfZjo9bVoHLenMDqjmnFE-BZpGC-iXMpkZg%3D%3D&gaa_ts=699bd3a7)

Today is February 21, 2026. The Trump administration recently fired James W Hundley from his position as interim US Attorney for the Eastern District of Virginia. Who had the president previously appointed to the acting attorney role just days after Erik Siebert resigned from the position?
(A) James W Hundley
(B) Letitia James
(C) Todd Blanche
(D) James Comey
(E) Lindsey Halligan
explanation: The article states: "Days after Siebert's exit, Trump appointed Lindsey Halligan, a White House aide and the president's former personal attorney, to the position."
model_output:
First, a quick summary of my reasoning: Based on web search results, Erik Siebert resigned as acting U.S. Attorney for the Eastern District of Virginia on September 19, 2025, under pressure from the Trump administration. Just days later (around September 21-22, 2025), President Trump appointed Lindsey Halligan, a former White House aide and one of his defense attorneys, as the interim replacement. This fits the question's timeline and details. Later events involved judges appointing James W. Hundley as interim, only for the administration to fire him quickly in February 2026, but the query specifically asks about the appointment right after Siebert's resignation.

<final_answer>
(E)
</final_answer>

===============================================

Today is February 21, 2026. NASA administrator Jared Isaacman announced on Saturday that the highly anticipated Artemis II lunar mission will almost definitely be delayed. What specific technical issue was cited as the cause for this expected delay?
(A) An interruption in the flow of helium required for launch operations.
(B) Hydrogen leaks detected in the rocket's seals and filters.
(C) A failure to fuel the rocket with 730,000 gallons of propellant over 50 hours.
(D) An issue with the space capsule's life support systems.
(E) A delay in the delivery of the SpaceX Starship lander.
explanation: The article reports: "But overnight on Friday, engineers observed an interruption in the flow of helium required for launch operations. 'This will almost assuredly impact the March launch window,' NASA said in a statement..."
model_output:
Based on recent news reports from a web search, the Artemis II mission delay announced by NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman appears to stem from a helium-related issue in the rocket, specifically an interruption or leak affecting helium flow. This has necessitated rolling the rocket back to the hangar for repairs, pushing the launch from March to at least April. While there were earlier mentions of hydrogen leaks during a wet dress rehearsal in early February, the specific cause cited for this latest delay is the helium problem, which aligns with option (A). Other options like (B) refer to prior issues, while (C), (D), and (E) don't match the reported details (noting that Starship is relevant to Artemis III, not II).

<final_answer>
(A)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- Al Jazeera ([https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/2/21/nasa-rules-out-march-launch-for-manned-moon-mission-over-technical-issues])
- Space.com ([https://www.space.com/space-exploration/artemis/problem-pops-up-with-artemis-2-moon-rocket-this-will-almost-assuredly-impact-the-march-launch-window])
- Facebook ([https://www.facebook.com/groups/SpaceLaunchSchedule/posts/1614052146301903])
- The New York Times ([https://www.nytimes.com/2026/02/21/science/nasa-artemis-helium-flaw.html])
- SpaceNews ([https://spacenews.com/nasa-preparing-for-artemis-2-rollback-to-fix-upper-stage-problem])
- NASASpaceFlight ([https://www.nasaspaceflight.com/2026/02/nasa-rollback-sls-delaying-artemis-april])
- BBC ([https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c626v265zqlo])
- DW.com ([https://www.dw.com/en/us-technical-issues-force-nasa-to-postpone-moon-mission/a-76075479])
- Bloomberg ([https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-21/nasa-likely-to-delay-moon-mission-after-newly-found-rocket-issue])

Today is February 21, 2026. As the US, Canada, and Mexico prepare for the upcoming USMCA trade deal review, US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer highlighted specific obstacles in negotiations with Canada. What specific action by Canada did Greer cite as a "big challenge" during an interview?
(A) Canada's refusal to lift the 35% tariff on American fentanyl and drug imports.
(B) The broadcasting of an anti-tariffs advert sponsored by the province of Ontario during the World Series.
(C) Canada's refusal to sell US wine and spirits on their shelves.
(D) The failure to integrate the North American economies under the 1990s free trade deal.
(E) The decision to prioritize trade ties with Mexico over the United States before the July 1 deadline.
explanation: The article quotes Jamieson Greer stating: "They continue to have certain barriers. They refuse to sell US wine and spirits on their shelves.,' he said. 'There are a variety of issues... and this makes it a big challenge and an obstacle for starting real negotiations with them.'"
model_output:
I reviewed recent news sources about US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer's comments on USMCA negotiations with Canada. In a February 11, 2026 interview, Greer specifically cited Canada's refusal to sell U.S. wine and spirits on their shelves as a key barrier, describing it as making negotiations "a big challenge."

<final_answer>
(C)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- [E&E News] (https://www.eenews.net/articles/us-trade-rep-says-canada-making-it-hard-to-begin-talks-on-usmca-review)[[1]](https://www.eenews.net/articles/us-trade-rep-says-canada-making-it-hard-to-begin-talks-on-usmca-review)

Today is February 21, 2026. NASA is aiming for an early March launch for its Artemis II mission after completing a successful "wet dress rehearsal". However, a previous rehearsal attempt in early February had to be aborted. What specifically caused the early termination of that first rehearsal?
(A) The crew was required to go into mandatory quarantine.
(B) An interruption in the flow of helium used to pressurize fuel tanks.
(C) Unfavorable weather conditions at the Kennedy Space Center.
(D) A hydrogen fuel leak at the launch pad.
(E) Issues with the Orion capsule's life support systems.
explanation: The text notes: "The first rehearsal, which took place at the beginning of February, ended early because of a hydrogen fuel leak at the launch pad."
model_output:
Based on the available information from NASA and related space news sources, the first wet dress rehearsal in early February 2026 was aborted due to a liquid hydrogen leak. This matches issues previously seen with the SLS rocket in earlier Artemis missions. A second rehearsal later in the month was successful, paving the way for the planned March launch.

<final_answer>
(D)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- [NASA] (https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/02/03/artemis-ii-wet-dress-rehearsal-test-terminated-at-t-515)[[1]](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/02/03/artemis-ii-wet-dress-rehearsal-test-terminated-at-t-515)
- [Yahoo News] (https://www.yahoo.com/news/articles/nasa-retest-artemis-2-moon-100000965.html)[[2]](https://www.yahoo.com/news/articles/nasa-retest-artemis-2-moon-100000965.html)

Today is February 21, 2026. Writer and philanthropist Anna Murdoch-Mann has died at the age of 81. During her 31-year marriage to Rupert Murdoch, she authored several novels. What was the specific subject of her 1988 novel, 'Family Business'?
(A) A memoir about her 31-year marriage to Rupert Murdoch.
(B) A fictionalized account of the 1969 abduction of Muriel McKay.
(C) A historical account of her time as a reporter for the Sydney Daily Telegraph.
(D) A chronicle of the succession battles between her children Elisabeth, James, and Lachlan.
(E) A multi-generational media dynasty.
explanation: The article states: "She penned several novels during their marriage, including the 1988 Family Business about a multi-generational media dynasty."
model_output:
The novel *Family Business* by Anna Murdoch (published in 1988) is a work of fiction that follows the McLean family across generations as they build and manage a newspaper and media empire, starting from a small-town paper founded by the protagonist's grandfather and expanding into a vast network involving corporate intrigue, family conflicts, and political elements. This aligns directly with option (E).

<final_answer>
(E)
</final_answer>

- Amazon [web:20] (https://www.amazon.com/Family-Business-Anna-Murdoch/dp/0688068588)
- Kirkus Reviews [web:22] (https://www.kirkusreviews.com/book-reviews/a/anna-murdoch-2/family-business-2)
- Sydney Morning Herald [web:25] (https://www.smh.com.au/national/rupert-murdoch-s-second-wife-and-glamorous-mainstay-anna-had-incalculable-influence-20240725-p5jwhp.html)

===============================================

Today is February 21, 2026. Actor Eric Dane has passed away 10 months after revealing his ALS diagnosis. Before his death, how did Dane specifically use his acting career to raise awareness about his condition?
(A) He appeared on an episode of 'Brilliant Minds' playing a firefighter struggling to accept an ALS diagnosis.
(B) He returned to the set of 'Euphoria' to film a final season centered around a character with motor-neurone disease.
(C) He starred as an on-screen surgeon with ALS in the medical drama 'Grey's Anatomy'.
(D) He produced and starred in a documentary video for Target ALS to raise $1bn in federal funding.
(E) He played a clinician in an Oxford University-sponsored film about the rapid progression of neurodegenerative brain disorders.
explanation: The text states: "He also used his craft to raise awareness, appearing on an episode of medical drama Brilliant Minds in November as a firefighter struggling to accept help after an ALS diagnosis."
model_output:
Based on the provided date of February 21, 2026, and the details in the query, I researched recent information about Eric Dane's ALS diagnosis and his efforts to raise awareness through his acting career. Web searches confirmed that Dane revealed his ALS diagnosis in April 2025 and passed away on February 19, 2026[[1]](https://people.com/everything-eric-dane-said-about-als-diagnosis-11910481). Further searches and browsing revealed that he took on a guest role in the NBC medical drama *Brilliant Minds*, playing a firefighter who receives an ALS diagnosis and struggles to share it with his family (the episode aired in November 2025)[[2]](https://katu.com/news/entertainment/eric-dane-is-bringing-his-als-advocacy-to-a-new-role-on-brilliant-minds). This role was explicitly tied to his real-life advocacy for ALS awareness, including pushing for legislative changes like the reauthorization of the ACT for ALS[[2]](https://katu.com/news/entertainment/eric-dane-is-bringing-his-als-advocacy-to-a-new-role-on-brilliant-minds). None of the other options (such as returning to *Euphoria*, appearing in *Grey's Anatomy* with ALS, producing a Target ALS documentary, or starring in an Oxford-sponsored film) matched the verified details from sources.

<final_answer>
(A)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- People.com (https://people.com/everything-eric-dane-said-about-als-diagnosis-11910481)
- KATU.com (https://katu.com/news/entertainment/eric-dane-is-bringing-his-als-advocacy-to-a-new-role-on-brilliant-minds)

Today is February 21, 2026. A woman was recently charged after abandoning her two-year-old dog at Las Vegas's Harry Reid International Airport. When questioned by police at the departure gate, what specific reason did she give for leaving the animal behind?
(A) She claimed she had arranged for the charity Retriever Rescue of Las Vegas to collect the animal.
(B) She stated the airline wouldn't let her fly with the dog and claimed it had a tracking device, implying it would return to her.
(C) She stated that she had lost the required documents for the golden doodle to fly as a service dog.
(D) She claimed the dog was aggressive and had to be left tied to a baggage sizer for passenger safety.
(E) She said she was fleeing a hostile situation and could not afford the fees to board the animal.
explanation: According to the police statement in the article: "When asked why she abandoned the dog, she stated the airline would not allow her to fly with it and claimed the dog had a tracking device - implying it was acceptable to leave the animal behind and it would return to her".
model_output:
According to reports, the woman was arrested after abandoning her goldendoodle at the airport. When questioned by police, she explained that the airline refused to let her fly with the dog and mentioned that it had a tracking device, suggesting she believed it would find its way back to her[[1]](https://ca.news.yahoo.com/woman-charged-over-abandoning-dog-155705298.html). This matches the details in option (B).

<final_answer>
(B)
</final_answer>

Today is February 21, 2026. The Supreme Court has struck down President Trump's use of the IEEPA for global tariffs. According to analysis of the ruling's political impact, why might some of Trump's conservative allies in Congress be "breathing somewhat easier" following this decision?
(A) Because they now have explicit authorization to draft a new tariff bill before the midterm elections.
(B) Because the ruling guarantees that tariff revenue will remain in the US Treasury instead of being refunded.
(C) Because Trump's tariffs and their costs to consumers have been unpopular, making Republican candidates vulnerable to Democratic attacks.
(D) Because the decision forces America's trading partners to make concessions ahead of the State of the Union Address.
(E) Because it allows them to push forward with efforts to end birthright citizenship and reshape the federal government.
explanation: The article explains: "In fact, some of Trump's conservative allies in Congress may be breathing somewhat easier with this decision. The president's tariffs - and the costs they have imposed on consumers - have been unpopular among many Americans. Republican candidates in battleground states and congressional districts would have been open to Democratic attacks for supporting Trump's policies."
model_output:
The scenario describes a Supreme Court ruling on February 20, 2026, striking down President Trump's use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to impose global tariffs. Based on available analysis of the political impact, the tariffs were broadly unpopular due to their economic effects, including higher costs passed on to consumers (effectively acting as a tax hike). This created political challenges for Republicans, especially in an election year, as Democrats could leverage the issue to attack GOP candidates. The ruling removes this liability, allowing conservative congressional allies to avoid defending the policy.

<final_answer>
(C)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- SCOTUSblog (https://www.scotusblog.com/2026/02/supreme-court-strikes-down-tariffs[[1]](https://www.scotusblog.com/2026/02/supreme-court-strikes-down-tariffs))
- Tax Foundation (https://taxfoundation.org/blog/supreme-court-trump-tariffs-ruling[[2]](https://taxfoundation.org/blog/supreme-court-trump-tariffs-ruling))

Today is February 21, 2026. The Supreme Court's decision to invalidate some of President Trump's emergency tariffs has shifted the global trade landscape. Despite the highly tariffed environment last year, what specific factor allowed China's trade to still flourish with the US?
(A) A massive pivot toward agricultural exports to the European Union and the UK.
(B) The collection of $240bn in tariff revenues directly from US-based manufacturers.
(C) American importers absorbing the costs of clothing and toys without raising prices.
(D) A surge in imports of IT hardware into the US amid the AI boom.
(E) Successful negotiations that exempted Chinese goods from the "Liberation Day" tariffs.
explanation: The article explicitly states: "But China has still flourished, thanks to a surge in imports of IT hardware into the US amid the AI boom."
model_output:
The scenario described appears to be set in a near-future context with ongoing US-China trade tensions under renewed tariffs. Based on recent reports, despite overall declines in some trade categories due to tariffs, China's exports to the US remained resilient in key sectors. Specifically, a surge in US imports of IT hardware from China—driven by high demand during the AI boom—allowed bilateral trade to flourish even in a highly tariffed environment.

<final_answer>
(D)
</final_answer>

Today is February 21, 2026. Following the Supreme Court's ruling against the IEEPA tariffs, President Trump has enacted new global tariffs using Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974. Under these new rules, what is the specific trade status of textiles and apparel imported from Costa Rica and Honduras?
(A) They are subject to a 10% tariff because the de minimis exemption was ended last year.
(B) They are subject to a 15% tariff as part of the new global temporary tariff structure.
(C) They are exempt from all tariffs solely because of the USMCA agreement.
(D) They face reciprocal industry-specific tariffs under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act.
(E) They remain duty-free under the Dominican Republic-Central America Free Trade Agreement.
explanation: The article notes: "Similarly, textiles and apparel from Costa Rica, the Dominican Republic, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras, and Nicaragua will remain duty-free under the Dominican Republic-Central America Free Trade Agreement."
model_output:
Based on the details of the temporary tariffs imposed under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, as outlined in the White House fact sheet, textiles and apparel from Costa Rica and Honduras are explicitly exempted from the new 10% ad valorem import duty[[1]](https://www.whitehouse.gov/fact-sheets/2026/02/fact-sheet-president-donald-j-trump-imposes-a-temporary-import-duty-to-address-fundamental-international-payment-problems). This exemption applies specifically to such articles that qualify for duty-free entry under the Dominican Republic-Central America Free Trade Agreement (CAFTA-DR). Therefore, they remain duty-free, which aligns with option (E). Other options do not match the details: for example, the tariff rate is 10% (not 15%), de minimis exemptions aren't referenced in relation to these goods, USMCA doesn't apply to these countries, and Section 232 isn't involved here.

<final_answer>
(E)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- White House Fact Sheet (https://www.whitehouse.gov/fact-sheets/2026/02/fact-sheet-president-donald-j-trump-imposes-a-temporary-import-duty-to-address-fundamental-international-payment-problems)

Today is February 21, 2026. The US government has collected roughly $130bn in tariffs under the now-struck-down IEEPA law, prompting discussions about refunds. According to Neil Bradley of the US Chamber of Commerce, what specific action will be "meaningful for the more than 200,000 small business importers"?
(A) Swift refunds of the impermissible tariffs for over 200,000 small business importers.
(B) The imposition of a 15% tariff under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974.
(C) Exempting critical minerals, metals, and pharmaceuticals from the new global levies.
(D) Pursuing a multi-year legal battle to prevent the government from reimbursing foreign entities.
(E) Encouraging businesses to invest and produce goods in the US rather than overseas.
explanation: The article quotes Neil Bradley saying: "Swift refunds of the impermissible tariffs will be meaningful for the more than 200,000 small business importers in this country and will help support stronger economic growth this year."
model_output:
Based on the query details, including the reference to the struck-down IEEPA tariffs and refunds, I searched for recent statements from Neil Bradley of the US Chamber of Commerce. Multiple sources directly quote him as stating that "swift refunds of the impermissible tariffs will be meaningful for the more than 200,000 small business importers" in the context of the Supreme Court's ruling on these tariffs[[1]](https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/feb/21/retailers-trump-tariffs-refunds)[[2]](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cn8z48xwqn3o)[[3]](https://thehill.com/business/5747752-chamber-commerce-supreme-court-tariff-ruling). This aligns precisely with option (A), while the other options appear to reference unrelated proposals or actions (such as new tariffs or exemptions) that aren't attributed to Bradley in this scenario.

<final_answer>
(A)
</final_answer>

Today is February 21, 2026. President Trump held a press conference shortly after the Supreme Court handed him a major setback regarding his global tariffs. How did the president specifically characterize the six justices who ruled against him?
(A) He said they were acting under the direct influence of the World Trade Organization.
(B) He said they were "just being fools and lapdogs for the Rhinos and the radical left Democrats."
(C) He stated they had committed an economic and national security disaster for the country.
(D) He said they lacked the strength, wisdom, and love of country needed to serve on the bench.
(E) He called their families an embarrassment and demanded their immediate resignations.
explanation: The article quotes Trump directly: "They're just being fools and lapdogs for the Rhinos and the radical left Democrats," referring to the six justices who struck down his tariffs.
model_output:
Based on the available information from recent news reports and transcripts of the February 20, 2026, press conference (noted as occurring shortly after the ruling, aligning with the query's February 21 reference), President Trump directly characterized the six justices in a statement that matches option (B). He praised the three dissenting justices (Thomas, Alito, and Kavanaugh) for their "strength and wisdom and love of our country" but criticized the majority as unpatriotic, a disgrace, swayed by foreign interests, and specifically as "just being fools and lap dogs for the rhinos and the radical left Democrats." He also called his own appointees Gorsuch and Barrett an "embarrassment to their families" for ruling against him, but the most specific characterization matching an option is the "fools and lapdogs" quote.

<final_answer>
(B)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- [Roll Call Factba.se](https://rollcall.com/factbase/trump/transcript/donald-trump-press-conference-supreme-court-tariffs-february-20-2026)[[1]](https://rollcall.com/factbase/trump/transcript/donald-trump-press-conference-supreme-court-tariffs-february-20-2026)

Today is February 21, 2026. President Trump has stated he is considering a limited military strike on Iran to pressure its leaders over their nuclear program. According to analysis, what specific domestic political risk does a protracted, multi-week conflict with Iran pose for the Trump administration?
(A) It would lead to the immediate impeachment of the president due to narrow Republican majorities in Congress.
(B) It could provoke a direct military retaliation against the USS Gerald R Ford and USS Abraham Lincoln.
(C) It would derail ongoing negotiations with US Special Envoy Steve Witkoff in Geneva.
(D) It threatens to unite the liberal members of the Supreme Court against his executive powers.
(E) It threatens alienating parts of Trump's Maga base who supported his promise to end messy foreign entanglements.
explanation: The article states: "...a protracted, multi-week conflict with Iran threatens alienating parts of Trump's Maga base which were drawn to his campaign promise of an end to messy foreign entanglements."
model_output:
Based on recent analyses from sources like Israel Hayom, a key domestic political risk of a protracted conflict with Iran for the Trump administration is the potential alienation of his MAGA base. This stems from Trump's 2024 campaign promises to avoid endless foreign wars and entanglements, which resonated strongly with his supporters. Prolonged military involvement could be seen as a betrayal of that stance, eroding support among Republicans who prefer swift, limited actions over open-ended engagements[[1]](https://www.israelhayom.com/2026/02/22/trump-iran-strike-midterm-elections-2026-republican-pressure). While other risks like economic fallout (e.g., inflation from disrupted oil supplies) and midterm election pressures are noted, the voter base issue is highlighted as particularly acute in the context of domestic politics.

<final_answer>
(E)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- [Israel Hayom] (https://www.israelhayom.com/2026/02/22/trump-iran-strike-midterm-elections-2026-republican-pressure)

===============================================

Today is February 21, 2026. Following the US Supreme Court's decision on tariffs, Canadian officials are preparing for the upcoming review of the USMCA trade pact. What did US-Canada Trade Minister Dominic LeBlanc specifically affirm about the agreement while speaking to reporters in Mexico?
(A) That both countries remain absolutely committed to a trilateral trade agreement.
(B) That Canada plans to double its non-US exports by 2035 if the review fails.
(C) That the fentanyl tariffs on Canada and Mexico were entirely unjustified.
(D) That Washington would prefer separate bilateral deals with Canada and Mexico.
(E) That negotiations would kick into high gear ahead of a 1 July deadline.
explanation: The article mentions: "In Mexico this week, LeBlanc told reporters that both countries 'remain absolutely committed to a trilateral trade agreement'."
model_output:
First, a quick note on the context: Dominic LeBlanc is Canada's minister responsible for Canada-U.S. trade relations (not "US-Canada Trade Minister" as phrased in the query, but that's a minor detail). Based on recent reporting from his trade mission and comments in Mexico around February 18, 2026, he was addressing the upcoming USMCA review amid tariff concerns and potential shifts under the U.S. administration.

His specific affirmation, as reported, was a commitment (on behalf of Canada and Mexico) to maintaining the trilateral structure of the agreement. This directly aligns with option (A), based on his quoted statements like: "We both remain absolutely committed to the trilateral free trade agreement and working together as this review process unfolds."

The other options don't match his reported affirmations:
- (B) Relates to export diversification plans, but that's not what he affirmed here.
- (C) Tariffs were discussed in broader coverage (e.g., exemptions and rulings), but he didn't call fentanyl tariffs "entirely unjustified" in this context.
- (D) This reflects reported U.S. preferences or fears, not LeBlanc's affirmation.
- (E) Negotiations are indeed ramping up for the 2026 review (with a July 1 joint statement deadline), but that's not the specific affirmation highlighted in his Mexico comments.

<final_answer>
(A)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- [Reuters][[1]](https://www.reuters.com/world/canada-mexico-want-trilateral-agreement-under-usmca-review-canadian-minister-2026-02-18) (https://www.reuters.com/world/canada-mexico-want-trilateral-agreement-under-usmca-review-canadian-minister-2026-02-18)

Today is February 21, 2026. The Supreme Court's invalidation of President Trump's IEEPA tariffs has raised questions about the estimated $130 billion already collected. According to US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, why are these previously collected revenues considered to be "in dispute"?
(A) Because more than 1,000 businesses have already filed class-action lawsuits to reclaim them.
(B) Because the Supreme Court did not provide any instructions on refunds when striking down the tariffs.
(C) Because the US international trade court ruled that small businesses must be refunded before large corporations.
(D) Because combining Section 122 tariffs with Section 232 and 301 tariffs will result in unchanged tariff revenue.
(E) Because Democratic Governor JB Pritzker successfully obtained a court order for $1,700 refund cheques per household.
explanation: The article notes: "Speaking at an event in Dallas, Bessent said revenues already brought in through the IEEPA tariffs were 'in dispute' since the Supreme Court did not provide any instructions on refunds."
model_output:
The revenues are considered "in dispute" according to Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent because the Supreme Court's ruling striking down the IEEPA tariffs did not address or provide guidance on what should happen with the money already collected, leaving the matter unresolved and potentially subject to further litigation in lower courts. Bessent has commented directly on this, noting that the Supreme Court "didn’t even address that" and describing any potential refunds as "the ultimate corporate welfare."[[1]](https://nypost.com/2026/02/22/us-news/scott-bessent-demurs-on-plans-to-refund-trumps-ieepa-tariffs-as-admin-rolls-out-plan-b)[[2]](https://finance.yahoo.com/news/bessent-says-tariff-refund-ultimate-223942102.html) This aligns with broader reporting on the ruling, which confirms the justices did not specify whether importers are entitled to refunds.[[3]](https://www.ttnews.com/articles/tariff-ruling-refunds-fight)

<final_answer>
(B)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- [New York Post] (https://nypost.com/2026/02/22/us-news/scott-bessent-demurs-on-plans-to-refund-trumps-ieepa-tariffs-as-admin-rolls-out-plan-b)
- [Yahoo Finance] (https://finance.yahoo.com/news/bessent-says-tariff-refund-ultimate-223942102.html)
- [Transport Topics] (https://www.ttnews.com/articles/tariff-ruling-refunds-fight)

===============================================

Today is February 21, 2026. Anna Murdoch-Mann, who passed away on February 17, finalized her divorce from media baron Rupert Murdoch in 1999. In an exclusive interview given after her separation, what specific prediction did she make regarding her ex-husband's media empire?
(A) That Lachlan Murdoch would eventually take full control of the news group.
(B) That the empire would face financial ruin due to one of the most expensive separations in history.
(C) That there would be a lot of heartbreak and hardship over the succession.
(D) That Wendi Deng would exert undue influence over the company's board of directors.
(E) That her children would permanently sever ties with the family business to avoid pressure.
explanation: The article states: "In an exclusive interview after her separation, Murdoch-Mann told Australian Women's Weekly, 'I think there's going to be a lot of heartbreak and hardship' over the succession for Murdoch's media empire."
model_output:
Based on the available information from searches and page browsing, the exclusive interview in question appears to have been conducted by journalist David Leser and published in the Australian Women's Weekly in 2001 (two years after the 1999 divorce). In it, Anna Murdoch-Mann discussed various topics related to her marriage, divorce, and family, including the pressures on her children and the future of the Murdoch media empire. The specific prediction she made that aligns with the question is captured in this direct quote from the interview: "But I think there’s going to be a lot of heartbreak and hardship with this [succession]."[[1]](https://www.smh.com.au/business/companies/anna-murdoch-told-me-succession-would-bring-heartbreak-how-right-she-was-20240919-p5kbz4.html) This matches option (C) verbatim. While she also expressed a desire for her children to pursue paths outside the family business and avoid the associated pressures[[1]](https://www.smh.com.au/business/companies/anna-murdoch-told-me-succession-would-bring-heartbreak-how-right-she-was-20240919-p5kbz4.html) (which has some thematic overlap with option (E) but is not an exact match), the explicit prediction highlighted in reports about the interview centers on the heartbreak and hardship of succession.

<final_answer>
(C)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- [SMH] (https://www.smh.com.au/business/companies/anna-murdoch-told-me-succession-would-bring-heartbreak-how-right-she-was-20240919-p5kbz4.html)
- [The Guardian] (https://www.theguardian.com/media/2001/jul/25/pressandpublishing.citynews1)
- [BBC] (https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cp8150xj36wo)

Today is February 21, 2026. Former 'Grey's Anatomy' star Eric Dane has died from ALS, a disease for which there is currently no cure. According to Professor Kevin Talbot of the University of Oxford, what is the primary reason why a cure for ALS has not yet been found?
(A) The rapid progression of the disease makes it impossible to administer experimental clinical trial drugs.
(B) Federal funding for ALS research has been consistently blocked by government bureaucracy.
(C) Pharmaceutical companies prioritize treating more common diseases like Alzheimer's and cancer.
(D) The condition does not have a single unifying cause.
(E) Patients typically refuse to take part in the necessary clinical research studies.
explanation: The article details: "Medical treatments and technologies can improve the quality of life of those with ALS, but no cure has been found. Professor Kevin Talbot, who runs the University of Oxford's Nuffield Department of Clinic Neurosciences, told the BBC this is because it does not 'have a single unifying cause'."
model_output:
According to recent reporting on Eric Dane's death from ALS, Professor Kevin Talbot of the University of Oxford stated that no cure has been found because the disease does not have a single unifying cause.

<final_answer>
(D)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- [BBC] (https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c24d9vpjmnmo)[[1]](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c24d9vpjmnmo)

Today is February 21, 2026. In addition to broad global levies, President Trump has made other recent changes to US trade rules. Last year, the president ended a specific policy known as the "de minimis exemption". What exactly did this exemption allow?
(A) An exemption that allowed goods from Canada and Mexico to avoid the fentanyl tariffs under the USMCA.
(B) A policy that permitted critical minerals and pharmaceuticals to be imported without reciprocal tariffs.
(C) A rule that shielded small businesses from pursuing costly refunds for unlawful IEEPA taxes.
(D) A diplomatic agreement that kept tariff levels at an average of 6% for European imports.
(E) A rule that allowed goods valued at $800 or less to enter the US without paying any tariffs.
explanation: The article concludes by noting: "At the same time, Trump said he is keeping up tariffs on low-cost goods. Last year, he ended what was known as the de minimis exemption, which had allowed goods valued at $800 or less to enter the US without paying any tariffs."
model_output:
Based on the details of the de minimis exemption in US trade policy (which was indeed ended in 2025 under President Trump, per recent reports), the correct answer is (E). This matches the rule that previously allowed low-value shipments (under $800) to enter tariff-free, often exploited in e-commerce.

<final_answer>
(E)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- DHL (https://www.dhl.com/discover/en-us/global-logistics-advice/logistics-insights/the-end-of-de-minimis-exemption-meaning-for-your-business)[[1]](https://www.dhl.com/discover/en-us/global-logistics-advice/logistics-insights/the-end-of-de-minimis-exemption-meaning-for-your-business)
